g.data-narration: Welcome to Biomedical Waste Management - an essential topic for understanding healthcare safety and environmental protection.
Biomedical waste includes all waste from healthcare activities that may pose risks to human health and the environment.
g.text: Any waste generated during diagnosis, treatment, immunization, or research that may pose
risks to human health and the environment. Only 10-25% is infectious (BMW).

g.data-narration: Improper biomedical waste management causes disease spread, soil contamination, water pollution, and poses serious health hazards.
BMW Rules 2016 categorize waste into four color-coded types: Yellow, Red, White (sharps), and Blue - each with specific handling.
g.text: Four Categories of Biomedical Waste (BMW Rules 2016)

g.data-narration: Yellow Category includes human anatomical waste, soiled items, discarded medicines, and chemical waste requiring special disposal.
Red Category contains contaminated recyclable waste like plastic tubes, bottles, and glass items that can be treated and recycled.
White Category (Sharps) includes needles, syringes, blades, and broken glass that can cause puncture injuries and transmit infections.
Blue Category includes metallic implants, glassware, and other wastes requiring specialized disposal methods separate from other categories.
Primary sources include hospitals, nursing homes, diagnostic labs, blood banks, and research centers - the main generators of biomedical waste.
Secondary sources include dental clinics, veterinary hospitals, home healthcare, medical camps, mortuaries, and pharmaceutical industries.
Segregation at source is critical - waste must be separated into color-coded containers immediately where generated to prevent contamination.
g.text: Segregation at Source - Color Coded Containers

g.data-narration: A 100-bed hospital generates 100-150 kg of BMW daily. Formula: Total beds × 1-2 kg = daily waste. Proper segregation reduces costs by 80-90%.
Key points: BMW has 4 categories (Yellow/Red/White/Blue), segregation at source is critical, 100-bed hospital generates 100-150 kg/day.
Remember: Yellow (anatomical), Red (recyclable), White (sharps), Blue (metallic/glass). Know the generation rate and treatment methods.
